You Don't Have to Read Clean Code to Write Decent Code

This is a slightly unusual post — it took me close to a year to write. The reason it took that long is because it's a collection of things I've seen from students during my time as a teaching assistant. I'm not pasting their code verbatim — I clean and adapt each example so nobody gets singled out. The goal is just "here's how you could write this better".

This isn't a Clean Code book recap — it's not going to teach you naming theory or refactoring patterns top-down. It's me sharing actual code patterns I've seen, with notes on why they hurt readability.
I'm also going to skip the heavily theoretical concepts. The vibe is "you could write this a bit better", not "let me lecture you".
Everyone has their own code style, so this isn't a "you must do it my way" post. It's more "here's what I'd suggest, and why".
And I'm a Vue dev, so some of the examples lean Vue.
OK, time to see if your code is one of the things I'm about to call out.

These meaningless names are weirdly common.
What's a better name? Look at what the function/variable is actually doing. Since "do something" doesn't help, let me fill in:
This is an AJAX function talking to a backend. We can figure that out by reading the body — but the function name tells us nothing. You have to read the whole thing.
Avoid that by naming with intent:
Now anyone reading the file knows this function "fetches products" without reading the body. Sticking with
forces every reader to do extra work, which isn't a great habit.
Variables matter just as much. In the example above the data goes into
Looks fine — but you don't know what
Clear naming tells the reader what's inside without making them guess (or run the code).
Simple rule for naming variables and functions — long and readable beats short and cryptic.

Avoid ambiguous names — you don't want anyone cursing at your code.
Tangent — I once saw a magical pattern from someone wondering why every page crash happened:
forever — instant browser crash. Spending a few seconds thinking about names avoids stuff like this.
Avoid pinyin (or local language transliterations) in code
I once had a student name functions and variables using Mandarin pinyin:

I wouldn't recommend this — if you ever hand the project off to a non-Mandarin-speaking developer, they'll have no idea what's going on. And most devs default to English naming, so this becomes friction. Stick with English.
(If your English is weak, Google Translate works.)

Following the previous one — JavaScript has all three (
in the same file just confuses readers about scoping. For example:
Modern browsers actually block this now (I think it used to work), and you'll see something like this in the console:
Uncaught SyntaxError: Identifier 'a' has already been declared
Uncaught SyntaxError: redeclaration of let a
So to avoid the problem entirely, don't mix
. (Also: don't earn yourself an awkward "let's grab coffee" chat with a coworker.)

const user = {
  name: 'Ray',
  age: 18,
  address: 'Taipei',
  email: 'example@gmail.com',

  updatedAt: '2019-01-01 00:00:00',
};

  name: user.name,
  age: user.age,
  address: user.address,
  email: user.email,
  phone: user.phone,
  isDeveloper: user.isDeveloper,
  isEnabled: user.isEnabled,
  category: user.category,
  isMale: user.isMale,
  isFemale: user.isFemale,
  loginType: user.loginType,
  avatar: user.avatar,
  createdAt: user.createdAt,
  updatedAt: user.updatedAt,

Six imports from the same module. Maintenance-hostile. Group them:
Lots of JS devs drop the braces on
That's syntactically fine — JavaScript implicitly adds the braces — and it's OK for a single line. But once you chain
Already feels cluttered. A junior dev has to take a few seconds to parse it. With braces:
More vertical space, but the structure is way clearer.
(Also: you're not writing Python — stop with the indentation-only style.)
That said, I'm not saying never drop braces. Plenty of real projects use the one-line form. Just don't over-do it.
Good comments help you remember intent. Too many comments hurt readability:
// declare a variable called products with an empty array
// use axios to fetch products from https://example.com/products
// put the response into the products array

Comments outnumber code. They don't help — they make the file harder to read. Code is its own commentary. If the code is hard to read, fix the code, not the comments. Save your effort for code clarity. Unless the logic is genuinely complex.
I've also seen Vue code like this:
comment? The hook below it is actually

. So is the comment wrong, or is the comment trying to flag something meaningful? Now I have to go ask the original author to find out. Fine if they're still on the team — but if they've left, the comment is a useless mystery. Avoid this.
// import getProducts from './getProducts'; // remember to uncomment
I'm genuinely lost. The line is already commented — why do you need a comment to remind yourself to comment it out? Use comments deliberately, not as TODO notes that decay.
While we're here — go remove obsolete commented-out code. If you know a line is dead, delete it. Don't leave it sitting there.
Don't smush everything onto one line
I've seen someone literally collapse multi-line code into a single line:
Please don't try to optimize for line count manually. That kind of compression should be done by
, or your bundler — not you. Doing it by hand just kills readability.
The ternary operator is great — but easy to over-use. Like this:
In practice, keep ternaries to simple conditions. For complex logic, an
is the universal debug tool — but please remove them before shipping. Style guides usually flag
as a dev-only construct, and there are good reasons: you might accidentally leak sensitive info, and beyond that, a codebase full of stray logs reads like the author didn't finish their work.
Use whitespace deliberately — but don't go overboard
I once reviewed an assignment that looked like this:
A 100-line file ballooned to over 500 lines because of vacuous blank lines between every statement. Reading it took infinite scrolling.
The opposite is also bad — no blank lines anywhere:
Blank lines used deliberately make code easier to read. Just don't carpet-bomb your file with them.
If it loops, write a loop
In real projects, repetitive blocks are everywhere. I once saw a student hand-write twenty
If you're using a framework, use its loop construct. In Vue,

Either is better than hand-writing 20 options.
Don't mix Composition API with Options API
This one's the bread and butter of student Vue3 final projects. People mix Composition API and Options API in the same component — what I call "ComOption API" (kidding):

<script>
export default {
  data () {
    return {
    }
  },
  mounted () {
    // ...
  },
  setup() {
    return {
      // ...
    }
  }
}
</script>
Yes really, I see this a lot. It runs, but please don't.
Why? The lifecycle hooks are different. Options API uses
. The two writing styles are also very different. Imagine inheriting a codebase that mixes both.
React has the same situation — class component vs. function component — also not great to mix.
That's most of what I've seen as a TA. Hopefully these examples help you write a bit better, and save you from the "let's grab a coffee" chat with a coworker.
These are guidelines, not rules. Every project has its own code style — some of the brace stuff in particular is context-dependent, and people
A lot of the above also gets caught by a linter. But it's worth building good habits before turning the linter on — that way when you do enable it, it doesn't look like a wall of red.

This post took me close to a year to write — it's a collection of real code smells I've seen in my time as a TA, anonymized and cleaned up. Not a Clean Code book recap; more like "here are common patterns that hurt readability, and how to write them so future-you (or your reviewer) doesn't suffer." Naming, var vs let/const, ES6 destructuring, comments, formatting, ternaries, loops, and not mixing Composition API with Options API.
